signals.title: NZD/CAD — G8 Bilateral Theme + RBNZ Bias
signals.text: NZD/CAD exhibits extreme G8 divergence as NZD leads the scorecard (+0.611%) while CAD lags (-1.076%). The 1.687% spread is driven by the RBNZ 2.25% hawkish bias (hike expected 27 May) vs BoC 2.25% neutral stance. NZD/USD COT LF net short -16,833 vs hike bias confirms a structural short-squeeze risk in NZD crosses. NZD/CAD upside remains the path of least resistance until the BoC meeting on 10 June.

signals.title: USD/JPY — Fed Dovish Shift + NFP Risk
signals.text: USD/JPY at 156.61 faces immediate pressure as Fed Governor Miran explicitly calls for rate cuts, contradicting the 'extended pause' narrative. This dovish pivot arrives ahead of April NFP (exp. +62k), threatening the 300bp Fed-BoJ carry advantage. JPY COT LF net short -88,543 remains a crowded trade vulnerable to a rapid unwind if US jobs data underwhelms. Watch for a break of 155.50 to confirm a trend reversal.

signals.title: AUD/USD — Positioning + Retail Divergence
signals.text: AUD/USD at 0.7245 shows a significant institutional vs retail divergence. COT LF net long +48,299 (74.8% long) indicates professional conviction, while retail sentiment is at an 88% short extreme. This contrarian setup suggests retail is unsuccessfully fading the 60bp AUD carry premium (RBA 4.35% vs Fed 3.75%). Retail capitulation risk accelerates the move toward 0.7350 if NFP triggers broad USD weakness.

signals.title: EUR/USD — Correlation Break + ECB Neutrality
signals.text: EUR/USD vs EuroStoxx correlation has shifted to +0.721 (z=+1.78σ), breaking the historical inverse relationship. EUR/USD at 1.1779 is now trading as a pro-cyclical asset rather than a funding currency. Despite the 175bp carry deficit (Fed 3.75% vs ECB 2.0%), EUR is catching a bid on equity strength. The move is capped by the ECB's hold bias; watch the 1.1850 resistance level ahead of the 11 June ECB meeting.

signals.title: USD/CHF — Retail Extreme + Carry Support
signals.text: USD/CHF at 0.7779 is testing retail patience with 85% of community traders positioned long. While the 375bp carry advantage (Fed 3.75% vs SNB 0.0%) structurally supports the pair, the extreme retail long concentration suggests a lack of fresh buyers. VIX at 17.1 (normal volatility) keeps safe-haven CHF demand dormant, but any spike in geopolitical tension would trigger a sharp long-liquidation toward 0.7650.
